title: Files with no safety net
content: Plain files in a data lake have limited safety guarantees. If a job crashes halfway through writing, you can end up with incomplete or inconsistent output. If someone reads at the wrong moment, they may see broken data. For serious analytical and production work, that is a major problem.
next: How do you make loose files behave like a real database?

title: Delta Lake's answer
content: Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer built on the popular Parquet file format, with one crucial addition: a transaction log. That log is the key that turns a pile of files into a reliable table.
next: What exactly does this log do?

title: The log is the hero
content: The transaction log is an ordered record of changes made to the table. A change only becomes part of the official table state once it is written to the log. A half-finished write does not become visible as a valid version, so readers avoid seeing incomplete results. This is the main mechanism that improves reliability.
next: What guarantees does that give you?

title: ACID comes to the lake
content: The log enables ACID-style guarantees, the reliability model long associated with databases. Atomicity means a change fully happens or not at all. Consistency and isolation reduce conflicts between readers and writers. Durability means committed changes remain recorded. Together, these rules make data operations safer and more predictable.
next: What does this feel like in practice?

title: Trust, restored
content: With these guarantees in place, lake storage behaves more like a database table and less like a loose file folder. Readers are less likely to encounter half-written data, and teams can trust table state more consistently. This reliability was one of the missing pieces the lakehouse model needed.
next: Can the log do more than just prevent corruption?

title: Travel back in time
content: Because the log records every version of the table, you can query it as it was yesterday or last week — and roll back a bad change in seconds. This 'time travel' makes mistakes recoverable and auditing easy.
next: What stops bad data from getting in to begin with?

title: A bouncer at the door
content: Delta enforces the table's schema: incoming data is checked against expected columns and types, and incompatible data can be rejected. This lowers the risk that a bad type or missing field silently damages downstream work. When a real schema change is intended, schema evolution supports that change in a more controlled way.
next: What about fixing or updating records that are already there?

title: Update, delete, and merge
content: Plain lake files were painful to change — practically append-only. Delta supports proper UPDATE, DELETE, and MERGE. MERGE (an 'upsert') updates existing rows and inserts new ones in a single, clean step — perfect for keeping a table in sync with a source.
next: Does it handle both scheduled and real-time data?

title: One table for batch and streaming
content: The same Delta table can support both scheduled batch updates and streaming-style ingestion. This unification reduces the need for separate storage patterns for different speeds of data arrival. In practice, it removes a significant amount of system complexity.
next: Do you have to opt in to all of this?

title: Open, and on by default
content: Delta Lake is open source, so your data isn't locked to one vendor. And on Databricks, every table is a Delta table by default. You get this reliability automatically, without doing anything special.
next: So how does it all fit back into the lakehouse?

title: The foundation of the lakehouse
content: Delta Lake is what makes the lakehouse real: it turns cheap, loose files into trustworthy tables that behave like a warehouse while keeping a lake's openness. Reliable storage, governance, and an easy platform are now all in place.
next: So what do companies actually build with all of this today?
